name: How do I budget for hidden problems in older Pacifica or Daly City bathrooms?

mainEntity.name: What kinds of hidden issues typically appear when opening up older bathrooms in Pacifica and Daly City, and how much contingency budget should I set aside to handle them without stalling the project?
mainEntity.text: What kinds of hidden issues typically appear when opening up older bathrooms in Pacifica and Daly City, and how much contingency budget should I set aside to handle them without stalling the project?

acceptedAnswer.text: In many older Pacifica and Daly City homes, bathroom walls and floors hide issues that only appear after demolition. Common surprises include water‑damaged subfloors, mold behind shower walls, unvented or undersized fans, old wiring without proper grounding, and plumbing done to outdated or non‑code standards. On coastal homes, long‑term humidity and previous minor leaks can make these more likely. Because these problems must be fixed while the bathroom is open, it is smart to build a contingency into your budget from the start. A common guideline is 10–20% of your total bathroom remodel budget reserved specifically for hidden conditions. In older or heavily DIY‑altered homes, lean toward the higher end of that range. Ask your contractor how they handle discoveries behind walls. Ideally, they will document conditions with photos, pause work in the affected area, provide options and pricing in a written change order, and obtain your approval before proceeding. Clarify whether they include any allowance in the base bid for minor framing or subfloor repairs. You can also reduce surprises by having a pre‑remodel inspection focused on moisture, structure, and electrical. While this cannot reveal everything, it can identify obvious risks and help you mentally and financially prepare, so hidden issues don’t derail the project halfway through.
acceptedAnswer.description: Older bathrooms in Pacifica and Daly City often hide water damage, mold, outdated wiring, undersized fans, and non‑code plumbing. Set aside a 10–20% contingency on top of your remodel budget to handle repairs when walls and floors are opened. Ask your contractor to show photos and change orders before proceeding so you understand each added cost.
